quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Nomenclature: Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, and Acids
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Roman Numerals for Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Acid Naming, Binary Covalent Compounds, Formula Writing

questions.question: What is the correct name for FeO?
questions.explanation: FeO is named iron(II) oxide. Iron is a transition metal with variable charge. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so iron must be +2 to balance. The Roman numeral (II) indicates the charge on the iron ion.

questions.question: What does the Roman numeral in iron(III) oxide indicate?
questions.explanation: The Roman numeral in iron(III) oxide indicates the charge on the iron ion (+3). This is necessary because transition metals can form ions with different charges.

questions.question: Which element keeps its name unchanged in compound naming?
questions.explanation: The metal keeps its name unchanged in compound naming. The non-metal typically ends with the suffix '-ide' (e.g., chlorine becomes chloride).

questions.question: What is the correct name for CuCl₂?
questions.explanation: CuCl₂ is named copper(II) chloride. Chlorine has a -1 charge, so two chlorines give -2. Therefore, copper must have a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: What is the correct name for H₂S (covalent form)?
questions.explanation: H₂S is named hydrogen sulfide. Although it is a covalent compound, it is traditionally named as if it were ionic (common name). The systematic covalent name would be dihydrogen sulfide, but hydrogen sulfide is widely accepted.

questions.question: Which compound is named 'sodium oxide'?
questions.explanation: Na₂O is sodium oxide. Sodium (Na⁺) and oxygen (O²⁻) combine in a 2:1 ratio to balance charges. NaO would be incorrect, NaO₂ is sodium peroxide, and Na₂O₂ is also sodium peroxide.

questions.question: What is the correct name for Cl₂O₇?
questions.explanation: Cl₂O₇ is named dichlorine heptoxide. The prefix 'di-' indicates two chlorine atoms, and 'hept-' indicates seven oxygen atoms.

questions.question: What is the correct name for PbO₂?
questions.explanation: PbO₂ is named lead(IV) oxide. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so two oxygens give -4. Therefore, lead must have a +4 charge, indicated by (IV).

questions.question: What is the formula for dinitrogen tetrafluoride?
questions.explanation: Dinitrogen tetrafluoride has the formula N₂F₄. The prefix 'di-' means two nitrogen atoms, and 'tetra-' means four fluorine atoms.

questions.question: The compound Cu₂O is named:
questions.explanation: Cu₂O is named copper(I) oxide. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so two coppers must total +2, meaning each copper has a +1 charge. Cupric oxide would be CuO (copper(II) oxide).

questions.question: Which element typically forms only one type of ion and does not require a Roman numeral?
questions.explanation: Sodium (a Group 1 metal) typically forms only one type of ion (+1) and does not require a Roman numeral. Iron, copper, and manganese are transition metals with variable charges requiring Roman numerals.

questions.question: Name Na₂SO₄:
questions.explanation: Na₂SO₄ is named sodium sulfate. It is an ionic compound containing the polyatomic ion sulfate (SO₄²⁻). No prefixes are used for ionic compounds.

questions.question: Name HNO₃:
questions.explanation: HNO₃ is named nitric acid. It is a common acid where the polyatomic ion NO₃⁻ (nitrate) combines with hydrogen. Acids with the '-ate' ending become '-ic' acid.

questions.question: Name HCl:
questions.explanation: HCl is named hydrochloric acid when in aqueous solution. As a gas, it is hydrogen chloride, but the acid form is hydrochloric acid. Binary acids use the prefix 'hydro-' and suffix '-ic'.

questions.question: Name H₂CO₃:
questions.explanation: H₂CO₃ is named carbonic acid. It contains the polyatomic ion carbonate (CO₃²⁻). The '-ate' ending becomes '-ic' acid.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered naming compounds with transition metals, polyatomic ions, and acids! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review Roman numeral usage and acid naming conventions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on identifying when to use Roman numerals and common polyatomic ions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review naming rules for transition metals, polyatomic ions, and acids.
